rationale: Both titles are strong and suit JACC well. Title X is polished and registry-anchored, emphasizing the patient population and registry provenance, but 'clinical and lead-related outcomes' is a somewhat broad descriptor that leaves the reader uncertain about the actual finding. Title Y is more informative because it explicitly states the comparative design (with vs. without jailed lead), signals a concrete finding (procedural success but more paravalvular leak), and still names the registry — giving readers the key result upfront, which is standard for high-impact journal titles. Title Y better satisfies the 'conveys key topic + finding + specifics' criterion.
loser_weakness: Title X does not communicate an actual finding or direction of effect; 'clinical and lead-related outcomes' is generic, so readers cannot gauge the study's main message without reading the abstract. The lack of a comparative frame also obscures the study design.
